rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical intramolecular halo-amination (iodo- and bromo-) of unactivated N-alkenyl sulfonamides to give 3-, 5-, and 6-membered halogenated N-heterocycles using LiI or LiBr as halide source and redox mediator.
electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of iodide (or bromide) to molecular I2 (Br2), which acts as the halogen-transfer mediator; the sulfonamide substrate itself is not oxidized at the anode (confirmed by CV). Cathode reduces protons to H2.

key_intermediates.name: molecular I2 generated in situ from anodic oxidation of I-
cyclic iodonium ion from alkene + I2 (intermediate I)

electron_flow: Anode oxidizes I- to I2; I2 reacts with the pendant alkene to form an iodonium ion, which undergoes intramolecular nucleophilic attack by the sulfonamide nitrogen to give the halogenated N-heterocycle with loss of H+; protons are reduced at the cathode to H2.

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction almost completely inhibited by 2.0 equiv TEMPO; starting material decomposed to 4-methylbenzenesulfonamide (detected by GC-MS); only trace 2a formed. Authors note this may reflect a radical pathway OR suppression via TEMPO-iodine adduct formation.

notes: CV shows two oxidation peaks for LiI (0.89 and 1.57 V vs Ag/AgCl) while sulfonamide 1a shows no anodic oxidation in the operating window, supporting iodide-mediator (not direct substrate oxidation) pathway. Authors favor an ionic iodonium/nucleophilic cyclization mechanism (Scheme 3), but acknowledge the TEMPO inhibition could alternatively implicate a radical pathway; they attribute it to TEMPO–I adduct formation rather than C-radical trapping.
